task:
[Instruction] You are given an excerpt from a script: 
 (opening:     The camera slowly zooms through a variety of insects and rocks. We follow a small yellow bug climb up a blade of grass.  As it spreads its wings to fly  we are whisked along the Australian outback and prairie by Ayers rock and eventually slow down as we approach Cody's house.)

(scene:  inside Cody's room.  The camera pans around to show Cody sleeping in his hammock.  The sound of Faloo's call is heard.  Cody hears it  jumps out of bed  and runs to the window.  He puts on his shirt and grabs his knife.)

(scene:  Cody sneaks past his mother who is in the kitchen listening to the radio.)

Announcer:    ... thundershowers are expected in the Crocodile Falls area and some of the surrounding gullies so take out your...

(scene:  Outside Cody's house.  Cody leaves the house  and closes the door behind him  but not quietly.)

Mom:     (from inside upon hearing the door) Cody!

Cody:    (whincing) Yeah mom?

Mom:     What about your breakfast?

Cody:    I've got some sandwiches in my pack.

Mom:     Well be home for supper.

Cody:    (hopping the gate) No worries mom.

(scene:  Cody runs toward the forest; Faloo's call is heard in the background.  He runs past some rock formations and enters the woods.  Birds follow him; and squak at him.)

Cody:    (to the birds) I know  I'm coming.
 (Cody jumps over a hollow log) Hustle up Nelson  Faloo's sounding the call!
 (Cody slides through a log  picks up a stick  and beats on the roof of the wombats home.) C'mon little wombats  hurry!
 (Cody continues to run through the forest with all of the animals following him.)
 (Cody arrives at the tree where Faloo has been sounding the call.)
 (to Faloo) Who's caught this time?

Faloo:   You don't know her  Cody  her name is Marahute  the great golden eagle.

Cody:    Where is she?

Faloo:   She's caught  high on a cliff in a poacher's trap.  You're the only one who can reach her.

Cody:    I'll get her loose.

Faloo:   Right-oh  hop on  no time to lose.
 (Cody hops onto Faloo and they travel through the forest and along a stream/river; more scenes of animals and the forest.)
 (They arrive at the cliff.)
 (pointing up towards the cliff) She's up on top of that ridge.  Be careful lit'l friend.

(scene:  various "time lapse" views of Cody climbing up the cliff.)
 (Cody reaches the top and sees the eagle.)

Cody:    Marahute!
 (Cody looks at the eagle; he approaches her slowly; she hears him and wakes up; Marahute screeches and struggles to get free.)
 (reassuring) Calm down  calm down.  I'm not gonna hurt you.  (Cody strokes Marahute on the head) That's a girl. Stay still... it's o.k.
 (Cody gets out his knife; Marahute sees the glint of the knife and    begins to struggle and scream)
 No wait!  I'm here to help you... easy!... easy!
 (Cody cuts two ropes.  Cody cuts the last rope to free Marahute.)
 You're free!!
 (As Marahute spreads her wings to fly  she knocks Cody off the cliff.)
 Aaaiigh!
 (Cody falls; Marahute dives down to catch him; she catches him just before he hits the ground; they begin to fly around; the animals see Cody on Marahute and stand in awe; Marahute files over several rock formations; the fly up above the clouds; Cody looks at his reflection in Marahute's eye.)
 Higher!
 (They fly even higher above the clouds; Marahute throws Cody and catches him; Cody is now held in Marahute's talons.)
 Woah!
 (Cody mocks an eagle screech; he laughs as Marahute tickles him; they cruise above the clouds which eventually open up to show the ground; Marahute nose dives towards the ground and a stream; she holds Cody just high enough above the water so that he is water skiing; they approach a flock of birds; Marahute lets Cody go and he skims through the birds  scattering them; Marahute grabs Cody just before he falls in and then put Cody right in front of her  on her beak (pushing him from behind); they go over the egde of a waterfall; Marahute catches Cody again; this time he rides by standing on her back; they arrive at Marahute's nest)
 Wow!
 (Cody and Marahute look at each other; Cody falls over as he attempts to look at Marahute upside down. Marahute moves some grass and feathers to show Cody her eggs)
 You're a mom!
 (Cody puts his ear to the eggs)
 They're very warm.  Are they gonna hatch soon?
 (Marahute ruffles her neck feathers in an affectionate manner; she sits on the eggs and then looks out "over her domain".)
 Where's the daddy eagle?  (Marahute drops her head) Oh... my dad's gone too.
 (Cody give Marahute an affectionate stroke;  as they fix the covering on the eggs  the wind picks up and blows a feather in Cody's face; he looks at it  plays with it  and puts it back.  Marahute picks it up and gives it to Cody and he gives her a hug.)
 (Marahute and Cody are now on the ground; Marahute takes off and Cody runs around making flying noises)

(scene:  just inside the forest.  A wanted poster of McLeach is posted on a tree; A mouse is tied up with a bell attached to it that rings as it struggles; Cody hears the bell and goes over to the mouse.)

Cody:    Heh heh... hey little fella  what happened to you?

Baitmouse:    (panicking) Oh no! No  no  no  no!!  Get away  get away! It's a trap  it's a trap.  Be careful  NO!

Cody:    (as the mouse is speaking) Don't worry  I'll get you loose.  Woah! (Cody falls into the trap.  He looks up to see a blinking light. 

[Requirement] Based on this, continue the scene and develop the storyline. The continuation must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.

 Taraji P. Henson revealed her son was 'racially profiled' by police officers .
Claims police stopped her son Marcel at University of Southern California .
USC's Public Safety boss says he has experienced racial profiling himself .
John Thomas has vowed to carry out his own investigation into the matter  . 

[Requirement] Write an article based on this summary. The article must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.

response:
Taraji P. Henson Speaks Out After Son Allegedly Racially Profiled by Police at USC

In a deeply personal revelation that has sparked national conversation about racial bias in law enforcement, acclaimed actress Taraji P. Henson has come forward with disturbing allegations that her son was subjected to racial profiling by police officers during an encounter at the University of Southern California. The Emmy-winning star of "Empire" and Oscar-nominated actress broke her silence on the incident, describing a frightening experience that she says exemplifies the ongoing struggles faced by young Black men in America, even in spaces that should represent opportunity and academic advancement.

The incident allegedly involved Henson's son, Marcel, who found himself stopped by police officers while on the campus of one of the nation's most prestigious universities. According to Henson's account, the encounter left her family shaken and served as a painful reminder that success and status offer no protection against the pervasive issue of racial profiling that continues to plague communities of color across the United States.

The University of Southern California, located in the heart of Los Angeles, has long prided itself on being a diverse and inclusive institution of higher learning. With a student body representing all fifty states and numerous countries around the world, USC has worked to cultivate an environment where students from every background can pursue their academic dreams. However, Henson's allegations have cast a harsh spotlight on the gap between institutional aspirations and the lived realities of Black students navigating campus life.

John Thomas, the head of Public Safety at USC, has responded to these serious allegations with a level of personal engagement that has surprised many observers. In an unexpected twist, Thomas revealed that he himself has experienced racial profiling, bringing a unique perspective to his role as the university's top security official. This disclosure has added a complex layer to the unfolding story, suggesting that even those in positions of authority are not immune to the indignities and dangers of discriminatory policing practices.

Thomas's acknowledgment of his own experiences with racial profiling represents a significant departure from the typically defensive posture that institutions often adopt when confronted with allegations of bias. Rather than immediately dismissing Henson's concerns or hiding behind bureaucratic language, Thomas has indicated that he understands intimately the pain and frustration that such encounters can cause. His personal connection to the issue has informed his response, which includes a commitment to conducting a thorough investigation into the circumstances surrounding Marcel's stop.

The investigation promised by Thomas will likely examine multiple aspects of the incident, including the initial reason for the stop, the conduct of the officers involved, and whether proper protocols were followed throughout the encounter. For Henson and her family, this investigation represents an important step toward accountability, though many critics of law enforcement practices remain skeptical about whether internal investigations can truly deliver justice in cases involving racial bias.

Henson's decision to speak publicly about her son's experience carries significant weight given her platform and influence. As one of Hollywood's most recognizable and respected actresses, she has the ability to draw national attention to issues that might otherwise be ignored or dismissed. Her willingness to share this personal family trauma demonstrates the depth of her concern and her belief that silence only perpetuates the problem.

The actress has built her career on portraying strong, complex Black women, from her breakout role in "Hustle & Flow" to her iconic character Cookie Lyon on "Empire." Through her work, she has consistently highlighted the experiences of African Americans in ways that resonate with mainstream audiences while maintaining authenticity. Now, she brings that same courage to her real-life advocacy, using her own family's painful experience to illuminate a systemic issue.

Racial profiling, the practice of targeting individuals for suspicion of crime based on their race or ethnicity rather than on individual behavior, has been extensively documented as a persistent problem in American law enforcement. Despite decades of research demonstrating its ineffectiveness as a crime-fighting tool and its devastating impact on communities of color, the practice continues in various forms across the country. For young Black men in particular, the experience of being stopped, questioned, and searched without legitimate cause has become an all-too-common feature of daily life.

The setting of this alleged incident—a university campus—makes it particularly troubling. Higher education institutions are meant to be places where young people can develop their potential and prepare for future success. When students of color are made to feel like outsiders or suspects in these environments, the damage extends beyond the immediate incident to affect their sense of belonging and their ability to fully engage with their educational opportunities.

Marcel's experience, as described by his mother, illustrates how racial profiling can intrude upon even the most privileged spaces. The son of a successful actress, attending or visiting a major university, would seem to have every advantage working in his favor. Yet the alleged stop suggests that stereotypes and bias can override such factors, reducing individuals to their perceived racial identity regardless of their actual circumstances.

The response from USC's Public Safety department under John Thomas's leadership will be closely watched as a test case for how universities can address allegations of racial bias within their security operations. Thomas's personal commitment to investigating the matter, informed by his own experiences with profiling, offers a model for institutional accountability that acknowledges the reality of bias while working to address it.

However, the effectiveness of any internal investigation depends on its transparency and its willingness to confront uncomfortable truths. History is filled with examples of institutional investigations that served primarily to protect the institution rather than to deliver genuine accountability. For Henson and other advocates, the true measure of USC's response will be whether it leads to meaningful changes in policy and practice.

The broader implications of this incident extend far beyond one family or one university. At a time when the nation continues to grapple with questions of racial justice and police accountability, Henson's revelation contributes to an ongoing conversation about the pervasiveness of bias in American institutions. Her status as a celebrity does not insulate her from these issues; if anything, her willingness to speak out underscores how universal the experience of racial profiling has become for Black families across the socioeconomic spectrum.

For young people like Marcel, encounters with law enforcement can shape their understanding of their place in society and their relationship with authority. Negative experiences, particularly those involving perceived unfairness or bias, can erode trust in institutions and contribute to a sense of alienation that persists long after the specific incident has passed. Universities, as institutions dedicated to the development of young minds, have a particular responsibility to ensure that their security practices support rather than undermine their educational mission.

The intersection of celebrity and social justice has become increasingly prominent in recent years, with public figures using their platforms to draw attention to issues of systemic inequality. Henson joins a growing number of entertainers who have spoken out about racial justice, from her "Empire" co-star Jussie Smollett's controversial claims to the more substantiated experiences shared by athletes, musicians, and other actors. While celebrity involvement can sometimes distract from the underlying issues, in this case Henson's personal connection to the experience lends authenticity to her advocacy.

The psychological impact of racial profiling on its victims has been well-documented by researchers. Beyond the immediate fear and humiliation of being stopped without cause, individuals who experience profiling often report lasting effects including anxiety, hypervigilance, and altered behavior patterns designed to avoid future encounters. For parents like Henson, the knowledge that their children face these risks regardless of their achievements or circumstances can be a source of ongoing stress and grief.

Thomas's investigation will need to address not only the specific incident involving Marcel but also the broader culture and practices of USC's Public Safety department. Meaningful reform requires looking beyond individual bad actors to examine the systems and assumptions that enable biased policing to persist. This includes reviewing training programs, stop and search policies, and mechanisms for accountability when misconduct is identified.

The location of the alleged incident in Los Angeles adds another dimension to the story. The city has its own complex history with police-community relations, including high-profile cases of excessive force and racial bias that have shaped local politics and activism for decades. USC's position within this urban context means that its security operations exist in relationship with larger patterns of policing in the city, patterns that have often been criticized for their disproportionate impact on communities of color.

As the investigation proceeds, Henson has indicated that she will continue to advocate for accountability and change. Her willingness to share her family's experience, despite the personal pain involved, reflects a belief that public attention is necessary to drive progress on these issues. For other families who have experienced similar encounters without the platform to demand accountability, her voice may help to validate their experiences and sustain pressure for reform.

The outcome of this case will be significant not only for Henson and her son but for the broader movement against racial profiling in American institutions. If USC, under Thomas's leadership, can demonstrate a genuine commitment to identifying and addressing bias in its security operations, it could serve as a model for other universities facing similar challenges. Conversely, a superficial or defensive response would reinforce skepticism about the possibility of institutional self-reform.

In the end, Taraji P. Henson's revelation about her son's experience at USC reminds us that the struggle against racial bias remains urgent and ongoing. Even as progress has been made in some areas, the persistence of profiling in spaces that should be safe and welcoming demonstrates how deeply embedded these patterns remain. The response to her allegations, both from USC and from the broader public, will be a test of our collective commitment to creating a society where young people can pursue their dreams without fear of being targeted because of their race.

Conversely, a superficial or defensive response would reinforce skepticism about the possibility of institutional self-reform.

In the end, Taraji P. Henson's revelation about her son's experience at USC reminds us that the struggle against racial bias remains urgent and ongoing. Even as progress has been made in some areas, the persistence of profiling in spaces that should be safe and welcoming demonstrates how deeply embedded these patterns remain. The response to her allegations, both from USC and from the broader public, will be a test of our collective commitment to creating a society where young people can pursue their dreams without fear of being targeted because of their race.
